content.body:
Hi all, attached is a brief state of the art review regarding current implementations of the *allow-once* option in major browser vendors' permissions system. 

**TL;DR**: All major vendors already implement it, including Chrome and Chromium-based projects (which inherit the functionality), Firefox, and Safari. Therefore, if we do include it as a requirement (for market access essentially), it wouldn't be too disruptive.

I do believe it's worthy of requirement-status (for sensitive permissions, obviously not clipboard or notifications), especially in light of the rise in agentic web browsers.

Happy to discuss
content.formatted_body:
Hi all, attached is a brief state of the art review regarding current implementations of the allow-once option in major browser vendors' permissions system.

TL;DR: All major vendors already implement it, including Chrome and Chromium-based projects (which inherit the functionality), Firefox, and Safari. Therefore, if we do include it as a requirement (for market access essentially), it wouldn't be too disruptive.

I do believe it's worthy of requirement-status (for sensitive permissions, obviously not clipboard or notifications), especially in light of the rise in agentic web browsers.

Happy to discuss

content.body: Seems good, and consistent with CRA with regard to (e) protect confidentiality, (g) data minimisation and (j) limit attack surfaces.

yes, it very aligned with several CRA requirements as well as the CRA's risk-based logic itself. 

Realistically, I don't believe we will face pushback from any major browser vendor for including this.

Additionally, the CRA's drafting preceeds the rollout of agentic web browsers, and is therefore not necessarily designed for the implications that come with it. Even though the CRA is not an AI-specific regulation in itself, i believe the commission will appreciate forward-looking requirements like this. 

In any case, even without the risks of agentic web browsing, i still believe this should be included.

This quick review is excellent, a good model for lots of other situations. Seems like we have good evidence that an “allow-once” requirement would not be disruptive to the market. It is necessary to explain why it is required by the CRA, but I think it fits within 1.1.g data minimization pretty clearly
content.formatted_body: This quick review is excellent, a good model for lots of other situations. Seems like we have good evidence that an “allow-once” requirement would not be disruptive to the market. It is necessary to explain why it is required by the CRA, but I think it fits within 1.1.g data minimization pretty clearly

content.body: With respect to agentic browsing, I am not sure what kind of point you are trying to make. Agentic browsing introduces lots of new risks, and we agreed in this draft that we aren’t going to treat them yet. The risks cut across many areas and may relate to permissions, which may mean additional requirements *for that case*. In drafting today, I would prefer if we focus on what comes up in the non-agentic case.
content.formatted_body: With respect to agentic browsing, I am not sure what kind of point you are trying to make. Agentic browsing introduces lots of new risks, and we agreed in this draft that we aren’t going to treat them yet. The risks cut across many areas and may relate to permissions, which may mean additional requirements for that case. In drafting today, I would prefer if we focus on what comes up in the non-agentic case.

content.body: It is just really hard to forward-project anything about agentic browsing. I don’t know how to do it
content.formatted_body: It is just really hard to forward-project anything about agentic browsing. I don’t know how to do it
